_disclosure: Correct/flawed judgments below were made by the benchmarking model (Claude) reading each translation against the English reference, not by a human annotator or automated metric (no BLEU/COMET run). Treat as directional, not ground truth.
_methodology_note: Two separate axes were judged because a single axis was misleading: (1) content_accuracy = are the facts/meaning preserved regardless of grammatical mood; (2) mood_preserved = did the translation keep the imperative/command register of instruction-style prompts (Escribe/Explica/Menciona = 'Write/Explain/Mention'), rather than collapsing it into a third-person description ('He writes/It explains'). Argos systematically fails axis 2 while remaining fine on axis 1.

flawed.issue: imprecise: 'certification review'/'test drill' instead of 'certification exam'/'practice exam'
drops 'Road' from 'Silk Road trade routes', slightly generic
'cliente' mistranslated as 'customer' instead of 'client' (wrong technical term)

mood_preserved.flawed_pattern: Consistently converts Spanish imperative verbs (Escribe, Explica, Menciona, Describe, Redacta, Organiza, Crea, Diseña, Da, Señala, Indica, Sugiere) into third-person declarative English (He wrote / It explains / It describes / He mentions / points to), turning an instruction into a description of one being carried out. Present in essentially every one of the 15 outputs to some degree.

flawed.issue: 'equipo' (team) mistranslated as 'equipment' — changes meaning of the sentence (team coverage vs. equipment coverage)
drifts to 'It describes the entire process...' instead of imperative 'Describe the entire process...'

summary: On raw factual/content accuracy the two backends are close (Argos 12/15, Google 14/15) — Argos (free, offline, open-source, CPU-only) holds up surprisingly well. The real gap is register: Google preserves the imperative/instruction mood of directive prompts in 13/15 cases, Argos does so in essentially none, systematically flattening commands into third-person descriptions. For LLM-prompt translation specifically (where the translated text IS the instruction sent to a model), that register collapse is a meaningful defect Argos users should know about — a translated 'prompt' that reads as a description of a task rather than a command may get materially different behavior from a downstream LLM.
